event: Prehistoric hunter-gatherers inhabit the region during the African Humid Period, when the Sahara was greener and dotted with lakes.
Neolithic peoples leave rock art and engravings across the western Sahara depicting cattle, giraffes, and other wildlife from a wetter savanna environment.
Progressive desertification of the Sahara forces populations to migrate and adopt pastoral and nomadic lifeways suited to an increasingly arid landscape.
Berber (Amazigh) pastoralist groups expand across the western Maghreb and Saharan fringes, ancestral to later Sanhaja confederations.
Carthaginian and Phoenician navigators reportedly explore the Atlantic coast of northwest Africa, with Hanno the Navigator's expedition skirting the region.
The introduction of the dromedary camel transforms Saharan life, enabling long-distance trans-Saharan trade and nomadic mobility.
Arab-Islamic expansion across North Africa begins the gradual spread of Islam toward the Saharan Berber tribes.
The Sanhaja Berber confederation controls trans-Saharan caravan routes linking the Maghreb with the gold-rich West African Sahel.
The Almoravid movement arises among Sanhaja Berbers of the western Sahara, launching a religious and military reform that will conquer Morocco and Muslim Spain.
The Almohad Caliphate supersedes the Almoravids, extending its influence over the western Saharan tribes.
The Maqil Arab tribes begin migrating into the western Sahara, gradually mixing with and Arabizing the local Sanhaja Berbers.
The Hassaniya Arabic dialect emerges from the fusion of Arab and Berber populations, becoming the language of the Sahrawi people.
Castilian and Norman expeditions reach the nearby Canary Islands, beginning sustained European contact with the adjacent Atlantic coast.
Portuguese navigators round the coast and capture captives near the Bay of Arguin, opening early Atlantic slave trading along the Saharan shore.
Spain establishes the fortified trading post of Santa Cruz de la Mar Pequeña on the Saharan Atlantic coast.
The Char Bouba war breaks out among western Saharan tribes, after which the religious Zawiya and warrior Hassan tribes dominate the social order.
Spanish forces lose their last foothold at Santa Cruz de la Mar Pequeña, ending early Iberian coastal presence for over a century.
The Treaty of Wad-Ras after the Hispano-Moroccan War grants Spain rights to re-establish a fishing settlement on the Saharan coast.
Spain proclaims a protectorate over the coast from Cape Bojador to Cape Blanc, founding the settlement of Villa Cisneros (Dakhla) as the Scramble for Africa begins.
The Treaty of Paris between France and Spain delimits boundaries between Spanish Sahara and French territories in the region.
Further Franco-Spanish conventions define the extent of the Spanish zone, dividing it into Saguia el-Hamra and Rio de Oro.
With the establishment of the French and Spanish protectorates over Morocco, Spain consolidates control over its Saharan territory.
Spain, with French assistance, fully occupies the interior of the territory and suppresses Sahrawi tribal resistance.
The Spanish Civil War disrupts colonial administration, with the territory drawn into the wider conflict between Republican and Nationalist forces.
Spain merges Saguia el-Hamra and Rio de Oro into the single province of Spanish Sahara, after the Ifni War with Moroccan-backed irregulars.
Phosphate deposits are discovered at Bou Craa, soon making the territory one of the world's largest phosphate reserves.
The United Nations General Assembly first calls on Spain to decolonize Spanish Sahara in accordance with the right to self-determination.
Sahrawi nationalist Bassiri founds the Harakat Tahrir, an early anti-colonial liberation movement.
Spanish forces violently suppress a Sahrawi demonstration at the Zemla Intifada in El Aaiun, and the activist Bassiri disappears in custody.
The Polisario Front is founded and launches an armed struggle against Spanish colonial rule with its first attack at El-Khanga.
The International Court of Justice rejects Moroccan and Mauritanian sovereignty claims, affirming Sahrawi self-determination, while Morocco's Green March sends 350,000 settlers into the territory.
The Madrid Accords see Spain withdraw and partition the territory between Morocco and Mauritania, prompting Sahrawi refugees to flee toward Algeria.
The Polisario Front proclaims the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic (SADR) as Spain formally ends its presence and war intensifies.
Mauritania renounces its claim and withdraws after military defeats, after which Morocco annexes the southern Tiris al-Gharbiyya sector.
Morocco begins constructing a massive defensive sand berm (the Wall) to seal off Polisario fighters from the populated and resource-rich west.
The Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic is admitted to the Organisation of African Unity, prompting Morocco's eventual withdrawal from the body.
Morocco quits the Organisation of African Unity in protest at the SADR's seating, isolating itself diplomatically on the continent.
Morocco completes the final and longest section of the 2,700-kilometre defensive berm, partitioning the territory militarily.
A UN-brokered ceasefire ends sixteen years of war, and the UN deploys the MINURSO mission to organize a self-determination referendum.
The Houston Agreement, brokered by James Baker, attempts to break the deadlock over voter eligibility for the referendum.
The Baker Plan proposes phased autonomy followed by a referendum, but it collapses amid rejection by the parties.
The Independence Intifada erupts with Sahrawi protests against Moroccan rule across El Aaiun and other towns.
Morocco proposes an autonomy plan for the territory under its sovereignty, while the Polisario insists on a referendum including independence.
Moroccan forces dismantle the Gdeim Izik protest camp near El Aaiun, an event seen by some as a precursor to the Arab Spring.
Tensions flare in the Guerguerat buffer zone near the Mauritanian border as Moroccan and Polisario forces face off over a road project.
The European Court of Justice rules that EU trade and fisheries agreements with Morocco cannot apply to Western Sahara without Sahrawi consent.
The Polisario Front declares the 1991 ceasefire over after clashes at Guerguerat, while the United States recognizes Moroccan sovereignty over the territory.
France formally endorses Morocco's autonomy plan for Western Sahara, following similar shifts by Spain and other states amid ongoing UN-mediated impasse.
